coredata.dc:title: Simulated enzyme catalytic strategy for surface-enhanced Raman scattering detection of methylmercury by using carbon dots/gold-based nanozyme
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qiulan
Li, Kexiang
Liu, Jiaxin
Chen, Dan
Yang, Dezhi
Yang, Yaling
Li, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Methylmercury (MeHg) as an organometallic is much more toxic than inorganic mercury. Nevertheless, building an organometallic assay remains a great challenge due to the coexisting inorganic metal ions. Herein, we synthesize a gold nanozyme (NP-CDs/AuNPs) via using norepinephrine-based carbon dots (NP-CDs) as reducing agent. As expected, NP-CDs/AuNPs exhibits enhanced peroxidase-like activity in the presence of MeHg to form Au-Hg amalgam (Au@HgNPs). Compared with the NP-CDs/AuNPs, the formed Au@HgNPs could oxidize weak Raman-active reporter o-phenylendiamine (OPD) into the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity 2,3-diaminephenazine (DAP) with a single characteristic SERS peak of C-Hg bond at 477 cm−1. The results of UV–vis–kinetics and SERS-kinetics models demonstrated the better affinity between Au@HgNPs and the substrate than that of NP-CDs/AuNPs, indicating the high sensitivity SERS signal intensity response. The SERS-enhancing mechanism explicitly revealed that the generation of charge transfer complex Au@HgNPs further increases the “hot spot” effect. The work provides guidance in designing the ultrasensitive and specific analysis of organometallic compound via SERS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methylmercury
Nanozyme
Au-Hg amalgam
Peroxidase-like activity
Surface-enhanced Raman scattering

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of MeHg by the formation of Au@HgNPs
The principle of SERS sensing MeHg
Sensitive and selective detection of MeHg
Detection of MeHg in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was greatly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC Grant No. 32302210 ) and Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202001AT070002 and 202201AU070117 ), Major Science and Technology Project of Yunnan Province (No. 202002AE320006 ), Science and Technology Key Programs of China National Tobacco Corporation Yunnan Company (No. 2020530000241037 ), Yunnan Province tobacco quality supervision and testing station general project (No. 20220001 ), and the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2021M20192118096 and 2021T20200097 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV−vis spectra of NP-CDs/AuNPs in the presence or absence of MeHg (a); TEM image of NP-CDs/AuNPs (b); TEM image of Au@HgNPs (c); XRD (d), and XPS spectra of nanozymes (e-j).

CAT (a), OXD (b) and POD-like activity (c) performance, absorption spectra of the different substrates in the presence of NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs; SERS spectra of different NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs system (d).
DFT simulated SERS and SERS spectra of NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs (a); DLS analysis for size measurement (b); zeta potential for surface charge measurement (c).

SERS spectra for Au@HgNPs-OPD system with the different concentration of MeHg from 0.5 to 105.5 µg L−1 at 477 cm−1 (a); linear curve of characteristic peak intensity and MeHg concentration (b); Selectivity of Au@HgNPs-OPD system for MeHg detection (c and d).
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs (a); the sensing of MeHg involving the Au@HgNPs (b).

head.ce:title: Simulated enzyme catalytic strategy for surface-enhanced Raman scattering detection of methylmercury by using carbon dots/gold-based nanozyme

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Science and Technology, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The author contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methylmercury (MeHg) as an organometallic is much more toxic than inorganic mercury. Nevertheless, building an organometallic assay remains a great challenge due to the coexisting inorganic metal ions. Herein, we synthesize a gold nanozyme (NP-CDs/AuNPs) via using norepinephrine-based carbon dots (NP-CDs) as reducing agent. As expected, NP-CDs/AuNPs exhibits enhanced peroxidase-like activity in the presence of MeHg to form Au-Hg amalgam (Au@HgNPs). Compared with the NP-CDs/AuNPs, the formed Au@HgNPs could oxidize weak Raman-active reporter o-phenylendiamine (OPD) into the surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) activity 2,3-diaminephenazine (DAP) with a single characteristic SERS peak of C-Hg bond at 477 cm−1. The results of UV–vis–kinetics and SERS-kinetics models demonstrated the better affinity between Au@HgNPs and the substrate than that of NP-CDs/AuNPs, indicating the high sensitivity SERS signal intensity response. The SERS-enhancing mechanism explicitly revealed that the generation of charge transfer complex Au@HgNPs further increases the “hot spot” effect. The work provides guidance in designing the ultrasensitive and specific analysis of organometallic compound via SERS.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is an extremely toxic heavy metal, and its methylated form (i.e. MeHg) is known to be carcinogenic and highly toxic, posing a serious threat to human health, even at very low concentrations [1–3]. In particular, MeHg is much more toxic than mercury because it has an amplification factor of up to 106 in the food chain, especially in aquatic species [4,5]. Thus, there is an urgent need for sensitive, specific and rapid detection methods for MeHg species. Nanozymes have promising applications in sensing methods. Most reported sensors are mostly used for inorganic mercury detection. Ding et al. [6] proposed a colorimetric sensor based on the aggregation of Hg2+ resistant gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). Long et al. [7] used the fact that mercury can complex with Au to form amalgams with high peroxidase-mimicking activity to design a colorimetric method for Hg2+ detection in the presence of a chromogenic substrate (3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, TMB). Only a limited number of existing protocols can selectively recognize organic mercury, which most likely due to their inability to resist interference from other metal ions [8].

SERS as a prospecting ultrasensitive spectral analysis technology have received attention owing to its high sensitivity and exclusive molecular vibrational information of the analytes [9,10]. The adsorption of molecules on the surface of rough precious metal nanoparticles, especially gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), will significantly amplify the Raman signal [11]. AuNPs, one of the most widely studied nanoparticles, has been regarded as the promising nanozymes for SERS analysis. Moreover, AuNPs possess catalytic properties with inherent enzyme-like activities [12,13]. However, weak enzymatic activity and poor colloidal stability of AuNPs hinder further application [14,15]. Several groups have demonstrated that the carbon dots (CDs) is beneficial for improving the catalytic activity and SERS signals of AuNPs [16,17].

Meanwhile, the interference of inorganic metal ions is also a problem to be solved in MeHg detection. The reported AuNPs for specific detection of MeHg are very scarce, most of them can only achieve Hg2+ detection [18]. Donati et al. achieved the colorimetric detection of MeHg by exploiting the formation of Au-Hg amalgam [19]. Liu et al. reported that Au/Ni-Fe LDH/rGO nanocomposite can detect MeHg with a concentration as low as 1 × 10−8 M via SERS technology [3]. These studies demonstrated that the enhancement of catalytic activity and SERS activity by the formation of gold amalgam is conducive to the selective analysis of metal ions or organometallics.

Herein, carbon dots-based AuNPs (i.e. NP-CDs/AuNPs) with weak peroxidase-mimicking activity was prepared by using the norepinephrine-based carbon dots (NP-CDs) both as reducing and stabilizing agent. Notably, the introduction of MeHg can result in a distinctly enhanced oxidase (OXD) and peroxidase (POD)-mimicking activity of the NP-CDs/AuNPs, attributing to the reduction of MeHg to metal (Hg0) to form Au@HgNPs. Interestingly, Au@HgNPs oxidized the OPD to form DAP with strong Raman signal at 477 cm−1 ( Scheme 1). Further density functional theory (DFT) calculations, zeta potential analysis, and DLS were also performed to disclose the underlying mechanism for the significant enhancement of the Raman signal.

NP-CDs was prepared through a one-step microwave method (Multiwave PM 60, Jena, German). Firstly, 27.6 mg of norepinephrine and 2.1 g of citric acid (CA) monohydrate were dissolved into 30 mL of deionized water. Secondly, 50 μL ethylenediamine (EDA) was added to form a transparent solution under the assistance of ultrasound. Thirdly, the solution was transferred to a Teflon reactor, heated to 180℃ within 1 min under 850 W microwave irradiation, and reacted for 60 min. After cooling to room temperature, the product was filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. Finally, the obtained product was stored at 4°C for using in further experiments.

Briefly, 40 μL of NP-CDs (0.2 mg mL−1) was added to the mixture solution including 200 μL HAuCl4 (1%) and 30 mL of deionized water. After stirred at temperature of 90℃ for 25 min, the obtained NP-CDs/AuNPs was store at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MeHg by the formation of Au@HgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: In a 1.5 mL sample flask, 50 μL of MeHg solution containing different concentrations (0.5–105.5 μg L−1) was added to 200 μL of aqueous NP-CDs/AuNPs. The solution was stirred and incubated for 10 min. After that, 250 μL PBS buffer (pH 7.0) containing 75 μL H2O2 (10 mM), 50 μL OPD (2.5 mM) were added and incubated for 2 min. Finally, the Raman spectra were collected.

In a typical POD-like reaction, the enzyme activity was assessed overall by colorimetric reactions using OPD as substrates. A reaction mixture containing 100 μL of 50 mM H2O2, 100 μL of 1 mg mL−1 NP-CDs/AuNPs or Au@HgNPs, was exposed to the varied concentrations of OPD (5 mM, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 350, 400, 450, 500 μL). Finally, the total reaction volume was maintained to 4 mL. The absorbances were measured by UV–vis at 421 nm at regular time intervals. A lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal was used to calculate the kinetic parameters (K m and V max):
1
v
=
K
m
V
max
×
1
S
+
1
V
max
where v and V max refer to the initial and maximal reaction velocity, [S] is the concentration of the substrate (TMB), K m is the Michaelis constant.

The catalytic reaction of OPD was assayed by the SERS spectral tests under the same conditions above. The kinetic experiments of nanozymes were studied by adding different concentrations of OPD. SERS intensities (477 cm−1) were measured at different reaction times (within 60 s). The Lineweaver-Burk plot of the double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten equation was also used to calculate the SERS steady-state dynamic parameters (K m and V max).

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using Gaussian 09 W and Gaussview 5.0 program at the level of Opt+Freq/B3LYP/6–31 g(d). The difference of SERS peak between the experimental and theoretical vibrational spectra of DAP was also compared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The principle of SERS sensing MeHg
ce:section.ce:para: Noble metal-based nanozymes can also serve as Raman-enhancing substrates to amplify SERS signals. NP-CDs/AuNPs, with weak enzyme catalytic activity and Raman activity, can catalyze the oxidation of OPD into the SERS activity DAP. Both the OXD-like, POD-like and Raman activity were obviously enhanced after the addition of MeHg [20]. The reducing NP-CDs/AuNPs can catalyze the reduction of MeHg to Hg0, which can form and cover the Au-Hg amalgam on the surface of NP-CDs/AuNPs [21]. Wang et al. and Lu et al. have reported that AuNPs could catalyze the reduction of Hg2+ to form Au-Hg amalgam [22,23]. Interestingly, it is discovered that NP-CDs/AuNPs is only able to reduce MeHg to Hg0 even in the presence of Hg2+, demonstrating the excellent selectivity towards the MeHg assay. Furthermore, the formation of the Au@HgNPs extraordinarily stimulate and promote the mimetic oxidase performance of nanozyme. The enhanced OXD and POD-like activity can be applied to oxidize weak Raman-active reporter OPD into the SERS activity DAP (Scheme 1).

The increase in the size of Au@HgNPs is accompanied by the formation of a rough surface that leads to a further increase in the intensity of SERS signal. On the other hand, a new SERS signal is observed at 477 cm−1 due to the newly-formed C-Hg stretching vibrations [24], so a new SERS sensing MeHg is developed. The reaction can be described by Scheme 1. The SERS signal (at 477 cm−1) was enhanced with increasing MeHg concentration.

The absorption spectra of Au@HgNPs and NP-CDs/AuNPs exhibited the absorption maximum at 557 nm and 549 nm, respectively, revealing the forming of Au-Hg amalgam ( Fig. 1a). As shown in Fig. 1b-c, NP-CDs/AuNPs was highly monodispersed (Fig. 1b). While the formation of Au@HgNPs amalgam was evident with the presence of large particle aggregates fused together after MeHg incubation (Fig. 1c). The HRTEM images and SAED diffraction patterns of NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs showed that the two lattice fringe spacings are 0.209 nm and 0.211 nm, respectively, which is in well agreement with the crystal plane spacing of Au(111) and the results of the XRD [25]. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDX) elemental mappings further demonstrated the presence of Au, Hg, C, N and O elements in NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs samples (Fig. S1 and S2).

In the FTIR spectra of NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs, the characteristic peaks located at 3491 cm−1, 1708 cm−1, 1397 cm−1, 1209 cm−1 and 775 cm−1 revealed the presence of OH, C-H, C-OH and CC groups, respectively (Fig. S3) [26]. In the XRD patterns of NP-CDs, NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs (Fig. 1d), typical peaks at 2θ = 38.9°, 46.5°, 66.2°, and 77.5° are assigned to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) [27], which are consistent with the results of HRTEM and EDX.

To further verify the role of MeHg, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out to analyze the surface composition of the NP-CDs/AuNPs substrate before and after reacted to MeHg (Fig. 1e). The characteristic peaks of NP-CDs/AuNPs at 285.0 eV, 400.3 eV, 532.6 eV and 84.1 eV are the responses for C, N, O and Au elements, respectively, and the typical characteristic peaks of Au@HgNPs at 285.0 eV, 399.9 eV, 532.5 eV, 84.0 eV and 99.8 eV are the responses for C, N, O, Au and Hg elements, respectively [28]. These results demonstrated the formation of Hg0 on the surface of NP-CDs/AuNPs. Each element contents are listed in Table S1 and S2 in the Supporting Information, which is also confirmed these results.

The high-resolution spectra of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Au 4f and Hg 4f are shown in Fig. 1f-j. The XPS peak of C 1s could be observed at 284.4, 285.5, 286.9, and 288.7 eV for NP-CDs/AuNPs, and at 284.2, 285.2 and 287.9 eV for Au@HgNPs (Fig. 1f), which belong to the C-C/CC, C-N and OC-O bonds, respectively. The binding energies C 1s bands with the induction of MeHg moved to the lower binding energy, indicating the increasing of electron cloud density. The characteristic peaks of NP-CDs/AuNPs at 398.1 and 399.9 eV, and Au@HgNPs at 399.8, 400.6, and 403.2 eV, are attributed to the pyrroline N and quaternary N conformations, respectively (Fig. 1g) [29]. In the O 1s spectrum, three peaks at 531.5, 532.6 and 533.2 eV in NP-CDs/AuNPs, and three peaks at 531.6, 532.5 and 533.7 eV in Au@HgNPs (Fig. 1h), are attributed to the -CO, OC-O- and -C-OH groups, respectively [30]. There is a shift to higher binding energies for the peak positions of N 1s, O 1s with the addition of MeHg, further revealing the increasing of electron loss [31].

The high-resolution Au 4f XPS spectrum was divided into two peaks: the binding energies of NP-CDs/AuNPs at 84.1 and 86.9 eV, and Au@HgNPs at 84.8 and 88.6 eV, are attributed to Au 4 f7/2 and Au 4 f5/2 of metallic Au(0), respectively, indicating the complete reduction of the Au precursor (Fig. 1i). Furthermore, there is higher binding energies occurrence with shift 0.7 and 1.7 eV for the Au 4f in the catalytic reaction, indicating the enhancing of electron loss [32,33]. The change in binding energy induced by MeHg is the largest, which implies that MeHg promotes the mimetic oxidase performance of nanozyme. As shown in Fig. 1j, the XPS measurements indicate that high binding energies of Hg 4 f bands are seen after the induction of MeHg (103.9 and 99.7 eV), which attribute to Hg 4 f7/2 and 4 f5/2 of C-Hg bonds, respectively [33].

The OXD- and POD-like activities were estimated by utilizing TMB, ABTS and OPD as the catalytic reaction substrates. The catalase (CAT)-like activity of the nanoenzyme was assessed by using the nanozyme to decompose H2O2 ligated to the Ti(IV)-H2O2 complex [34]. There was only a small increase in CAT activity ( Fig. 2a), demonstrating that MeHg introduction had a slightly effect on the CAT activity of the nanozyme. In Fig. 2b-c, TMB, ABTS and OPD can be oxidized in presence of H2O2 by NP-CDs/AuNPs or Au@HgNPs to generate the correspondent resultants of oxidized-TMB, -ABTS, and -OPD with the obvious absorption peaks at 654, 416 nm, and fluorescence emission peak at 564 nm, respectively [35]. These results indicated that the enzyme catalytic activity of Au@HgNPs is superior compared to NP-CDs/AuNPs. Fig. 2d shows that Au@HgNPs exhibited a strong SERS response to OPD at 477 cm−1, which belongs to the newly-formed C-Hg stretching vibrations [36]. Therefore, MeHg not only promoted the NP-CDs/AuNPs with much better catalytic activity but also exhibited an interesting SERS enrichment effect. These results demonstrated that the Au@HgNPs-based nanozyme possessed both POD and OXD-like and SERS activities property, which could be further explored for the SERS sensor of MeHg.

Here, UV–vis–kinetics and SERS-kinetics models were applied to monitor the catalytic efficiency of the POD-like reaction [37]. To evaluate the catalytic mechanism of the NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs, a steady-state kinetic reaction was performed by changing the concentrations of OPD while keeping other concentrations constant. To well known, enzyme-mimicking response usually generates on catalyst surfaces, while UV–vis–kinetics only show the oxidation of substrate molecules in the solution, and SERS-kinetics provide in situ survey of the actual enzyme-mimicking catalytic reaction on a catalyst surface. The maximum rate (V max) of NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs nanozyme were calculated as 5.00 × 10−5 M s−1and 3.74 × 10−4 M s−1 for OPD by UV−vis absorption measurement, 0.331 × 10−9 M s−1 and 9.183 × 10−9 M s−1 for OPD by SERS measurement, respectively (Fig. S4a–d; Table S4). The results of UV−vis absorption measurement were consistent with SERS measurements. The catalytic rate of Au@HgNPs was faster comparing to NP-CDs/AuNPs. As well as we know, the K m value reveals the affinity of the substrate for the enzyme, and a lower K m value exhibits a stronger affinity and vice versa. K m values of NP-CDs/AuNPs and Au@HgNPs nanozyme were calculated as 4.132 mM and 0.238 mM for OPD by UV−vis absorption measurement, 0.879 mM and 0.331 mM for SERS measurement, respectively, which indicated that the affinity between Au@HgNPs and OPD was stronger than that of NP-CDs/AuNPs. These results offered dependable witness for the high catalytic efficiency of Au@HgNPs, due to excellent adsorption capacities and a high sensitivity SERS signal intensity response. This may be attributed to the occurrence of more “active sites” and “hot spots” on the surface of the Au@HgNPs [38].

The SERS signal of OPD on Au@HgNPs nanozyme fairly indicated that oxidation of OPD could be observed by SERS signal test, which displays a strong SERS signal at 477 cm−1 only in the presence of MeHg. The SERS spectra of OPD-AuNPs and OPD-Au@HgNPs molecules are displayed in Fig. 2d; the typical bands of 581, 1034 and 1259 cm−1 are ring deformation, ring breathing and C-N stretching in the benzenoid, and DAP molecules also appear with increasing time. Meanwhile, one excessive strong Raman signal at 477 cm−1 is observed, which is increased with increas of MeHg concentration. This fact further confirmed that the SERS technique is fit for testing the enzymatic reaction of OPD, thus employing the quantitative assay of MeHg.

DFT calculations were carried out to reliably assign the Raman bands, and the vibration modes were identified (As shown in Table S4) [39,40]. It could be observed that most of the simulated SERS spectra were in good agreement with the experimental the Raman vibrations ( Fig. 3a). The results exhibited that the catalytic reaction of OPD-Au@HgNPs system displayed strong SERS signals.

A highly SERS signal was observed probably due to the electrostatic interactions between the positive charge MeHg and the negative charge the NP-CDs/AuNPs, resulting in the generation of “hot spots”. The SERS signals increasing of Au@HgNPs+ OPD+H2O2 system could be interpreted by the size and ζ potential of NP-CDs/AuNPs and MeHg (Fig. 3b and c). The formation process of Au@HgNPs nanozyme was explored during the oxidation of OPD with H2O2 by observing Au@HgNPs size change with time. The results showed that both OPD and MeHg were introduced to NP-CDs/AuNPs with an instant increasing in the size with time (0–15 min), revealing the information Au@HgNPs. This increasing in size could be seen with a integrate nanozyme system that contains all three compositions, like NP-CDs/AuNPs, OPD and MeHg.

At the same time, charge variation of NP-CDs/AuNPs with and without OPD and MeHg was explored by ζ potential test with time (Fig. 3c). The results revealed that NP-CDs/AuNPs fast changed positive charge in oxidation of the OPD and MeHg. An obvious variety of surface charge was seen with NP-CDs/AuNPs+ H2O2+MeHg and NP-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+H2O2+OPD. In addition, ζ potential of NP-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+H2O2+OPD is largest in comparison with NP-CDs/AuNPs and NP-CDs/AuNPs+MeHg+OPD systems, which is in accordance with similar sizes varieties from DLS measurement. These results explicitly showed the charge transfer complex resulted in the generation of Au@HgNPs to form the “hot spots” for SERS signals enhancing.

Based on these findings, a strategy was proposed to detect methylmercury by relying on NP-CDs/AuNPs to reduce methylmercury to metal (Hg0) to form gold amalgam (Au@HgNPs), which effectively catalyzes the oxidation of OPD to SERS activity (DAP) by exploiting its enhanced enzyme mimicking activity. The factors affecting SERS detection mainly include pH value, temperature, concentration of NP-CDs/AuNPs and reaction time. Fig. S5a-j show the optimal pH, temperature, the concentrations of NP-CDs/AuNPs, and the optimal conditions were 7, 30 ℃, 0.36 mg mL−1, and 10 min respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of MeHg
ce:section.ce:para: The Au@HgNPs nanozyme was used as efficient SERS substrates, which are investigated with OPD as the reporter probes. This strategy is based on the properties of MeHg-stimulated OXD and POD, which oxidize OPD to DAP, resulting in an enhanced SERS signal at the 477 cm−1 position with increasing methylmercury content ( Fig. 4a and b). The SERS signal increased from 8655 a.u. in the blank group to 149138 a.u., and the SERS enhancement factor (EF) value was 17.2. In the range of 0.5–105.5 µg L−1 (inset figure of Fig. 4a), a linear plot could be obtained based on the relationship between SERS intensity and MeHg concentration, and the regression equation was [MeHg]= 1303.24x+ 18347.73 (µg L−1) (R2 =0.9817). The detection limit was 0.12 μg L−1 (with a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3), which is well below the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) maximum allowable level of 1.0 mg kg−1 for methylmercury in meat and fish foods. As shown in Fig. S6, the SERS intensity of OPD at 477 cm−1 has a great mean value in which the standard deviation is 3.17 % (n = 10), demonstrating the satisfactory reproducibility. Compared to other colorimetric methods (see Table S5), our method exhibits good sensitivity to MeHg analysis.

To assess the selectivity of the proposed method, different ions (K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cl-, HCO3 -, CO3 2-, SO4 2-, NO3 - 10-fold concentration) and Hg2+ (same concentration) were added to aqueous solutions containing Au@HgNPs, H2O2 and OPD. As can be seen from Fig. 4c, compared to other ions, the reaction solution showed a significant SERS signal at 477 cm−1 when MeHg was added to the reaction solution. Meanwhile, Ag+ can also change the SERS of NP-CDs/AuNPs. When Cl- is added to the reaction system, Ag+ interference could be eliminated (Fig. 4c and d). Therefore, SERS sensing of Au@HgNPs can specifically identify MeHg in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MeHg in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: After spiking the ambient water samples with standard MeHg, the MeHg content was measured using the optimized SERS sensor. As shown in Table S6, the spiking (10, 50, 100 µg L−1) recoveries for MeHg were in the range of 106.48–120.69 %. These results indicated that the proposed method has a good application prospect for the analysis of MeHg in real samples.

In conclusion, a novel SERS nanozyme probe for MeHg assay was developed based on the formation of the Au-Hg amalgam to enhance the enzymatic mimetic activity of NP-CDs/AuNPs, which further oxidize weak Raman-active reporter OPD into the SERS activity DAP. The formation of Au-Hg amalgam could result in the generation of the “hot spots” for SERS signals enhancing, which can be used for quantifying the MeHg. Unlike the methods reported in the literature, our method not only illustrated the mechanism of enzyme activity regulation and SERS signal amplification, but also opened a novel universal strategy for Raman-inactive methylmercury. In this respect, this work provides new insights to organometallic compound via SERS.

Qiulan Li and Kexiang Li contributed equally to this work. Qiulan Li: Investigation, Methodology, Software. Kexiang Li: Investigation, Methodology, Software. Jiaxin Liu: Data curation. Dan Chen: Visualization. Dezhi Yang: Review and editing, visualisation, supervision, project administration, and funding acquisition. Yaling Yang: Review and editing, visualisation, supervision, project administration, and funding acquisition. Hong Li: Review and editing, visualisation, supervision, project administration, and funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was greatly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC Grant No. 32302210) and Yunnan Fundamental Research Projects (No. 202001AT070002 and 202201AU070117), Major Science and Technology Project of Yunnan Province (No. 202002AE320006), Science and Technology Key Programs of China National Tobacco Corporation Yunnan Company (No. 2020530000241037), Yunnan Province tobacco quality supervision and testing station general project (No. 20220001), and the Analysis and Testing Foundation of Kunming University of Science and Technology (No. 2021M20192118096 and 2021T20200097).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2023.111156.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
